Roof Work Over a Building That Never Closes

html:
Roofing Notrees' Legacy Oilfield Buildings and Battery Storage Site

Notrees started as a Shell Oil company camp along Highway 302, and though the camp housing is long gone, the area still carries two different kinds of large roof structures worth talking about separately: decades-old legacy oilfield buildings from a field that's never stopped producing, and a newer battery storage installation built alongside the area's wind generation.

A Shell Camp That Outlasted Its Housing

The Notrees field has been in continuous production long enough that the buildings serving it span several eras of construction. Camp housing came down decades ago as company towns fell out of favor, but the wells kept pumping, and the pump equipment sheds, gathering lines, and treating facilities built to service them mostly stayed in place, upgraded and patched rather than replaced wholesale.

That leaves us working on buildings with a real range of roof ages on any given visit, sometimes on the same lease. A treating building put up in one decade sits next to a meter shed added in another, and each carries the roofing choices and shortcuts typical of when it was built.

Some of the oldest structures still standing near Notrees carry roofing approaches you don't see much anymore, built-up systems patched with whatever asphalt product was on hand at the time, or early modified bitumen laid over decking that's been re-covered more than once. Before recommending anything on one of these older buildings, we check what's actually underneath the visible surface, because a quick recoat over a roof that's already had two or three layers added over the decades isn't always the answer.

The Battery Storage Site's Different Roofing Needs

Notrees is also known for a large battery storage installation built alongside wind generation in the area, and the buildings on that site are a different animal from a wellhead shed. Battery enclosures, inverter and switchgear housings, and control buildings need tighter environmental sealing than a pump shed, because moisture reaching sensitive electronics is a far more expensive problem than moisture reaching a valve.

Roof penetrations for HVAC systems serving climate-controlled equipment rooms are the detail we watch closest on this side of Notrees. A sealant failure around one of those penetrations doesn't just mean a stained ceiling tile, it means a chance of water reaching equipment that the whole site depends on.

Roof Calls We Handle Around Notrees

Between the legacy oilfield side and the energy infrastructure side, the calls we get here cover a wide range:

  • Rust-through on decades-old legacy treating building panels
  • Hail damage on switchgear building roofs at the storage site
  • Sealant failures around HVAC penetrations on climate-controlled equipment rooms
  • Wind-lifted panels on pump equipment sheds along Highway 302
  • Ponding at low-slope additions built onto older camp-era structures
  • Corrosion at roof penetrations near produced-water handling equipment

When wind or hail hits this stretch of Highway 302 hard, we treat it as a storm damage response across both types of site rather than waiting for individual calls to trickle in.

Wind Off the Open Highway 302 Corridor

The presence of wind generation right next to the battery storage site isn't a coincidence, and it says something about how much wind this stretch of Highway 302 actually gets. Flat, open terrain with nothing to slow a front down puts real uplift stress on any exposed roof, legacy or new, and fastener and seam condition matters more here than on a sheltered in-town building.

We build that wind exposure into every roof recommendation we make around Notrees, favoring fastening patterns and edge details rated for sustained exposure rather than whatever's standard on a calmer site.

Working Around Two Different Operators' Security Standards

Legacy oil leases and energy infrastructure sites in the same area often belong to entirely different companies with entirely different safety protocols. A wellsite visit might need a basic sign-in, while the battery storage site can require a more involved safety briefing given the equipment on the property. We coordinate both sets of requirements ahead of a visit rather than treating every stop the same way.

We also schedule around each site's own tolerance for downtime. A legacy pump shed can usually wait a day or two once it's tarped and stabilized, but a roof issue affecting a climate-controlled equipment room at the storage site gets treated as urgent regardless of how minor it looks from the outside, since the building's whole job is keeping conditions stable inside.

Questions We Get About Roofing Work Around Notrees

Is Notrees still an active oil camp?

The camp housing is gone, but the field itself has stayed in production, and legacy buildings serving it are still standing and still need roof attention.

What's different about roofing a battery storage building versus a wellsite shed?

Sealing standards. A battery or switchgear building needs tighter moisture control around HVAC penetrations because the consequence of a leak reaching electronics is much higher than a leak reaching a valve.

Do you handle emergency tarp calls at energy infrastructure sites?

Yes, and we prioritize any exposed electrical or control equipment first before moving to permanent repair.

How do you handle two operators' different safety requirements in the same area?

We confirm each site's specific access and safety protocol ahead of the visit so a crew isn't caught unprepared moving between a wellsite and the storage facility.

Why does a legacy treating building need different attention than a newer building?

Age and unknown repair history. Older buildings often carry patches and shortcuts from decades of ownership changes, so we inspect them more thoroughly before recommending a fix.
